narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one—about a 69% chance of not passing. Picture your story through its eyes: right at the top of the list was your first-year decile of 1.0, signaling to the model that early on, your grades placed you at the lower end compared to peers, setting a challenging tone from the start. That pattern held steady with your third-year decile also at 1.0, reinforcing a consistent struggle to climb higher in your school's rankings. Your LSAT score of 34 piled on next, as it fell short of the benchmarks that often light the way to bar success for others. Layered in there was your Asian background, which the model linked to patterns where similar students faced steeper odds in this arena. Your lower-middle family income added another hurdle, suggesting fewer resources to buffer the intense demands ahead. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 whispered of foundational gaps that might echo forward, while choosing full-time study and being a woman each nudged the outlook a bit dimmer in the model's view. Together, these pieces painted a picture of someone fighting an uphill battle against stacked historical trends, not a reflection of your potential or heart, but a caution based on what similar paths have shown before. It's not the end of your story—many have rewritten predictions like this with targeted changes—but this is the honest lens that led to our decision. If you share more about your study habits or plans, we could explore ways to shift that narrative. Hang in there; your determination is what models can't measure.
